data.title: Energy Transfer: Q4 Could Be A Turning Point
data.description:
ET Stock: Topline Growth Amid Mixed Q4

I last analyzed Energy Transfer LP Common Units stock (ET) on Jan 27 with an article titled “Energy Transfer: Why Super Investors Like It At $17.” The article focused on the latest disclosures filed by professional fund managers and concluded with a hold rating for ET stock. Since then, there have been a few new developments worth noting. In this article, I will focus on the top 3 on my list: the updates provided in its FQ4 2025 earnings report (ER), its latest dividend declaration, and also the changes in oil prices due to the evolving geopolitical conflicts, especially those involving Iran. These developments have led me to see the potential of 2025 Q4 as a turning point for the stock. Despite the various uncertainties and growth pressures facing the company, I see some encouraging signs that help to counterbalance the risks and make the stock a solid hold.

The first encouraging sign is the growth of topline reported in Q4 2025. To wit, the chart below summarizes the financial performance of Energy Transfer in recent quarters. As seen, the company has been facing revenue declines in recent quarters and often missed both consensus estimates on both lines. In 2025 alone, ET reported three consecutive quarters of declining revenue on a YOY basis: a -2.82% drop in FQ1 2025, a -7.17% drop in FQ2, and a -3.94% dip in FQ3. However, in Q4, the company reported revenue of $25.32B, marking a sharp 29.57% YOY increase that beat expectations by $1.28B.

ET Stock: Dividend Growth In Focus

The picture is not all rosy, though. Earnings still underperformed, with EPS dialing in at $0.25, compared to $0.29 a year ago, and falling below consensus by a large gap of $0.11. In tandem, the latest dividend declaration continues to show growth pressure. More specifically, the table below displays the dividend declarations from ET in recent quarters. On the positive side, the company kept maintaining its remarkable dividend growth trajectory, increasing its quarterly distribution from $0.3250 in February 2025 to $0.335 for the February 2026 payout ex-div date on Feb 6, 2026. With this new declaration, the stock’s current FWD yield stands at 7.1% at the price of this writing, quite attractive to dividend investors.

However, on the negative side, the new payout marks a YOY +3.07% increase and continues to show growth pressure. To better contextualize things, the table below outlines the dividend growth metrics for Energy Transfer and the overall sector in the past few years. As seen, ET’s growth rates have lagged behind sector medians in the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods. In the past 3 years, ET’s dividend growth averaged 9.83% CAGR, -8.15% below the sector’s 7.69% median rate. The 5Y CAGR growth rates lagged even more (by more than -21%). And its latest dividend growth rate of 3.07% is a far cry from its 3-year average CAGR.

ET Stock: Correlation With Oil Prices

Moving on to the changes in oil prices. The surging oil prices due to geopolitical conflicts do not benefit Energy Transfer directly because the partnership’s revenue is largely insulated from the underlying value of the commodities it transports. The vast majority of its segment margins are fee-based, meaning ET earns a fixed dollar amount per barrel or million cubic feet (Mcf) regardless of the spot oil price.

As a reflection, the following figure displays the correlation of ET and OXY’s cash from operations (CFO) with respect to the Brent Crude Oil Spot price in the past. I picked OXY in this comparison as OXY is a major oil producer and derives most of its profile from producing and selling oil; thus, its profit is more directly tied to oil prices. As seen in the top panel of the chart, ET’s CFO shows a lack of correlation with an average coefficient of essentially zero (0.094 to be exact) in the long term. In comparison, the correlation coefficient for OXY was much stronger, with a 10-year average of 0.573. Also note that OXY’s correlation has never dipped below zero in the past decade on a quarterly basis.

High oil prices can benefit Energy Transfer indirectly by incentivizing upstream producers to increase drilling activity, which ultimately drives the record volumes necessary to fill ET’s vast infrastructure. When oil prices remain elevated, producers in the Permian Basin and other key regions ramp up production to capture higher margins, leading to a direct surge in the demand for gathering, processing, and transportation services.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of valuation, ET is currently priced at a P/E ratio that is attractive relative to the sector but not relative to its own historical norms. As shown in the chart below, the stock currently holds an overall valuation grade of B+, reflecting these mixed valuation dynamics. For example, the company's FWD P/E ratio on a non-GAAP basis stands at 12.64 now, representing a significant -23.25% discount compared to the sector median of 16.48. However, despite being cheaper than its peers, this multiple represents an equally large premium to its own history. The FWD P/E ratio is 26.2% higher than its own 5-year average of 10.02x. Let me close by echoing a point I made earlier about its 7.1% FWD yield with the latest dividend announcement. As attractive as it is in absolute terms, this current dividend yield is actually slightly below the stock’s long-term average of 7.57%, as you can see from the following screenshot, serving as another sign of its valuation premium.

To conclude, this reexamination of ET was largely motivated by 3 changes since my last writing: its FQ4 2025 ER, its latest dividend declaration, and the changes in oil prices due to the conflicts involving Iran. Overall, I kept seeing a mixed picture with both positives and negatives. I see large odds for growth—both in terms of EPS and dividends—to be tempered in the next few years with the uncertainties of its capital projects, leverage, and future CAPEX investments (all elaborated in my last article). However, Q4 does reveal a few positives (in particular the topline growth and potential for more favorable oil prices) to offset these issues and thus maintain a well-balanced return/risk profile overall.

data.title: Kilroy Realty's Challenges Are Priced In (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Kilroy Realty Corporation (KRC) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 10% of their value. While it pays a large dividend, concerns continue to mount about office demand and the potential for declining occupancy. I last covered shares of Kilroy in July, rating the stock a “Sell” given concerns about its debt load, and since then, shares have dropped 17%, justifying my caution. With updated financials and with shares having dropped below my $33 price target, now is a good time to revisit Kilroy.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Kilroy Realty generated $0.97 of funds from operation (“FFO”), which was a penny below consensus as revenue declined by 5% to $272 million. For the full year, it generated $4.20 of FFO, down about 8.5% from 2024 levels. While revenue was down $14 million, property expenses were up 2% to $65 million. A challenge for the company is that the cost to operate an office building is fairly constant whether a building is 50% or 100% occupied. Declining occupancy is likely to pressure margins going forward. As a reminder, Kilroy primarily operates in legacy West Coast markets, though it also has a presence in Austin.

Occupancy was 81.6% in Q4, which was up 60 bps sequentially as it was able to pull forward start dates on previously leased space. Unfortunately, I expect this improvement in occupancy to be short-lived. It is also worth noting that while up sequentially, occupancy was down 220 bps from last year. Over the past year, we have seen a meaningful weakening in San Diego as occupancy fell by 550 bps to 83.7%; San Francisco also declined by 120 bps to 86.2%. LA was fairly stable at 75%, though this is a weak absolute level.

During the quarter, it leased 827k square feet of space, the best Q4 since before COVID. While it signed a substantial number of leases, new leases were 27% below prior levels on a cash basis, a strikingly large drop. This was due to two low leases, one replacing a bankrupt tenant and another on a single-tenant property to keep it occupied in the near term while Kilroy seeks out a long-term future for the building. Absent these, cash rents were still 2.6% lower.

Now, Kilroy has a fairly well-staggered pace of lease maturities, which limits the potential pace of decline in occupancy. About 8% of leases expire each of the next two years, and I generally expect KRC to release ~80% of maturities, limiting occupancy declines to about 1.5% per year. The backdrop will get more difficult in the 2030-2031 timeframe, but by then, development activity should help to offset pressures on the legacy portfolio to some degree.

One other challenge in renewing a lease is that office rents have deflated in many markets post-COVID. Kilroy estimates that in the LA and Bay Area markets, its rents are currently about 10% above market rates. In other markets, its rents are a bit below market levels, but overall, renewals are likely to be a headwind to cash flow and margins,

Given its West Coast footprint, Kilroy will be sensitive to the impact of AI. Frankly, the outcome here is highly uncertain. On the bright side, AI firms are leasing out more space as they build out their workforce. Their growth is a clear benefit to office demand. The question is how much of this growth cannibalizes other technology employment. In recent months, there has been extreme concern about the potential for AI to displace other software jobs. If AI creates fewer tech jobs than it replaces, it could be a net detractor from office demand. I view the outcome here as highly uncertain but am cautious that AI may prove a net headwind to demand and employment trends over the next 3-5 years.

Its major development project, Kilroy Oyster Point Phase 2 (KOP 2), is making progress, securing 316,000 sq. ft. of leases. This project now has a 44% lease rate. This project is focused on the life science sector and is now part of KRC’s stabilized portfolio. Just 3% of the footprint is occupied, and converting its leased space to occupied space quickly during 2026 is essential, as KRC generally only starts collecting revenue when the space is ready to be occupied. I expect occupancy to reach ~15% by year-end.

In Q4, it entered into an agreement to sell two properties for $210 million while closing a previously announced transaction for $61 million. Against these sales, it added a life science building in San Diego for $192 million. Kilroy has been fairly active in recycling capital and raising cash to support ongoing development work. For the full year, it sold $465 million of properties and acquired $395 million of assets. This is likely to continue, and KRC expects to complete $325 million of asset sales in 2026.

These sales are important because Kilroy has $4.6 billion of debt and $180 million of cash. That leaves it with an elevated 7.1x debt/EBITDA leverage, which is higher than I like to see, as I view ~6x as a better place to operate. Given the losses KOP 2 will generate this year, its leverage ratio will likely worsen on a stand-alone basis, which is why divestitures are essential to maintaining balance sheet sustainability. On the bright side, its debt is well-laddered, limiting near-term refinancing risk.

As you can see, though, its debt is relatively low-cost, as KRC borrowed wisely when rates were low. As it refinances this debt, we will likely see it roll over maturities at higher yields, which is likely to be a ~$0.05 headwind to annual FFO at least through 2028. Now, shares offer a 7.2% dividend yield. It retains about $130 million of FFO after its dividend, though I view KRC as having about $70 million of annual maintenance cap-ex needs. Still, this provides ~$60 million of retained cash flow. As such, I view the dividend as secure at least through 2027. Still, this elevated debt level and limited retained cash flow are why KRC has been active in disposing of assets to support development and acquisition activities.

Looking ahead, Kilroy provided guidance for 2026 that is unlikely to ease fears its business is a “melting ice cube.” It expects $3.25-$3.45 of FFO, which assumes a modest decline in same-property NOI of -1.5% to 0%, as occupancy will average just 76%-78%. Excluding KOP 2, occupancy will be 80%-81.5%, implying a tick down from Q4 levels. While management has highlighted an improvement in the leasing environment, it still is not sufficiently strong to offset the pace of lease maturities. Results also will be weighed down by a ~$25 million drag from KOP 2 and Flower Mart as these projects scale up. Their contribution should improve in 2027, though they will remain negative.

Frankly, the magnitude of the FFO decline guided to in 2026 was worse than I expected, reflecting the meaningful losses on KOP 2 and two adverse lease developments in Q4. Given its biggest lease for KOP 2 is not slated to occupy the site until late 2027, losses will continue here for some time, though at a lessening pace. Even for ex-KOP 2, FFO is likely to be below $3.50, or about $2.90-$3.00 after maintenance.

I believe KRC should trade no more than 10x FFO after maintenance, given we are likely to see a ~2% long-term decline as leases roll over and occupancy steadily falls. That points to a fair value in the $29-$30 area. This is a bit below where shares are today, but with its dividend, the total return opportunity is ~0%. This is not attractive, and I definitely would not buy KRC. However, I do not see sufficient downside to remain a seller. As such, I am moving shares to a “Hold.” On rallies past $33, I would exit KRC, but I would want shares to drop to the mid-$20s before buying, given secular pressures in the office sector.

data.title: Why China Is Less Vulnerable To The Strait Of Hormuz Than You Might Think
data.description:
China Needs The War In Iran To End Immediately... Right?

China is the world’s largest oil importer, and 50% of its imports come from Gulf countries through the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial chokepoint responsible for 25% of the world's maritime oil transportation. China is by far the country that benefits the most from it; in fact, 37% of that oil is directed toward it.

In light of this data, the situation seems pretty clear: China is doomed along with its investors. The country desperately relies on the Strait of Hormuz to sustain its huge and growing energy demand.

To give you some perspective, the Chinese primary energy consumption is more than that of the US and EU combined (almost 1/3 of the world's consumption). And it is growing rapidly. Therefore, blocking overnight 50% of the oil imports seems a severe blow for the Chinese economy, right? Well, not exactly. The situation is much more complex.

The aforementioned numbers are correct, but I purposely described just one part of the whole story, something that I saw in many analyses over the last days. In this article I am going to shed some light on China’s energy consumption and why it is less vulnerable to the Strait of Hormuz than you might think.

Numbers Must Be Contextualized, Always

First of all, China is the world’s largest oil importer, but that doesn’t imply that oil imports account for the majority of its energy consumption. The main reason why it holds this title stems from its huge manufacturing industry and a population of more than 1 billion.

In fact, oil's share of total energy consumption is ~ 18% (~ 27% including gas). Not a small number, but not even that concerning. Anyway, that ~ ~18% is the starting point that will lead us to understand the consequences of a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz in relation to China’s energy consumption.

Of that 18%, we have to exclude the domestic production, about 30% of the oil consumption. Therefore, oil imports have a weight of ~ 12% on total energy consumption. However, not all oil imports come through the Strait of Hormuz, just ~ 50% of them. And this last calculation brings us to the number that we were looking for: ~ ~6% (over 5 million daily barrels). Including gas imports wouldn’t change the situation that much; in fact, we would still be talking about a number that is less than 10%.

Overall, the fact that China relies so much on coal (52% of energy consumption) is a strong hedge against an oil supply shock in the Middle East. Iran depends on oil exports to China (87% of total exports), but not the other way around. First, because China has multiple partners to deal with (Russia above all), and second, China’s energy consumption is largely skewed toward coal and renewable energies; the latter have experienced a strong improvement over the last decade, and the current weight is comparable to that of oil consumption.

In other words, China relies on coal to sustain its huge manufacturing industry, and it is investing significantly in renewable sources for all the other scopes. Of course, to avoid misunderstanding, China is not yet independent from oil; otherwise, it wouldn’t take up 18% of the total consumption share. Its primary use is for logistics/transportation; after all, you can’t fill your car with coal, and the same goes for air and truck freight. However, we can objectively affirm that China is doing a great job to become less reliant on oil (we can’t say the same in terms of environmental pollution): it has evolved into the world’s largest EV market, and 50% of new cars sold are EVs. Electric airplanes are still utopian, but electric cars and trucks are already part of the present.

Final Thoughts

Overall, believing that China will suffer greatly from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is a rather superficial statement; we have to go deeper. Only 6% of energy consumption depends on oil imports coming from the Strait of Hormuz; that’s because China has always preferred other types of energy sources, coal and renewables above all. For example, the US is in a totally different condition: oil and gas combined amount to 72% of total energy consumption.

If China were in a situation comparable to that of the US, the Strait of Hormuz closure would have been a very serious problem (the US is a net exporter of fossil fuels, unlike China), but this is not the case. And just for information, the Strait of Hormuz is not even the most important strait for China: the Strait of Malacca is way more strategic, both in terms of oil imports and trade volume.

All this doesn’t mean that China is immune to persistent oil shocks, but it is not in such a terrible situation considering that it is the world’s largest oil importer. We have to put this statement in context with all the other energy sources and the size of the country.

Furthermore, China is a country that tends to be careful in depending too much on the others, especially when it comes to energy: they know that a country based on the manufacturing industry is nothing if it can’t power its plants (unfortunately, Germany realized this too late).

It is not a coincidence that China has bet so much on renewables over the last few years and has started to accumulate a huge amount of onshore crude inventories: a record high of 1.20 billion barrels in early January. Considering that China typically imports 5 million oil barrels on a daily basis coming from the Strait of Hormuz, if closed, it would take 240 days (34 weeks) to run out of all the oil inventories. According to President Trump, the war should last 4-5 weeks, so it is well below the Chinese oil inventory capacity.

What if the war lasts more than 34 weeks? That’s a major concern, but not only for China, for all the world. If anything, China is less of a problem compared to the European Union and Japan, both oil-dependent economies that don’t produce it. Oil takes up 37% of Japan’s energy consumption, and 91% of it comes from Gulf countries. Basically, a third of the country’s energy consumption is threatened by the US-Iran war, and it doesn’t surprise me that the Japanese stock market has suffered greatly since 27 February. China has many more levers to pull before getting severely hit by this war, and for now it is still well covered. In a few months things can change, but that will be everybody’s problem.

data.title: Target: Sales Remain Weak, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Target Corporation (TGT) released its Q4 results in the after-market hours, and my initial read was that the sales side of the ledger reflects much of the same as in past quarters. That is, for the 13th straight quarter, the retailer reported flat or declining sales.

I’ve provided continuing coverage on TGT in the past, and my sentiment has been primarily neutral due to a combination of the sales picture as well as the multitude of headwinds TGT has faced over the years.

This includes its centerpiece in cultural political battles, its elevated exposure to shrink, and its unfavorable discretionary positioning in inflationary environments, which has created lopsided promotional drag relative to other off-price retailers.

Today, I remain neutral because I believe the retailer is very much in a “prove it” mode, and I believe that new CEO Michael Fiddelke has his work cut out for him.

Fiddelke does seem to have the right mindset, with stepped-up investments in the storefronts. These investments, however, could tap into TGT’s profitability, which has recently been on the mend.

I would need to see tangible and positive results from these investments, and until that happens, I would be content remaining neutral on the stock and its prospects.

TGT Stock Key Metrics

My neutral sentiment on TGT is echoed across-the-board from the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants and the broader analyst communities alike. The quants do grade TGT positively on most metrics, including valuation, but TGT is currently sitting on a capital “F” on growth, and I believe this is warranted.

Over the last three years, for example, TGT is sitting on a negative 1% CAGR for revenues. This compares to 5% for nearby peer Walmart (WMT).

Sentiment among the analyst communities is along the same lines.

In addition, from a price target perspective, analysts actually see some downside risk to the shares, with most valuing shares at the $115/share level, down about 5% from today’s $120/share price point. In my view, I believe TGT is valued appropriately at current trading levels.

TGT Stock Operating Performance

I’ve provided more negative remarks on TGT so far, but if there was one bright spot in the quarter, it was profitability.

While sales declined again, marking yet another quarter of pressure, TGT did manage to grow adjusted operating income and adjusted EPS in Q4 despite the revenue drop. That’s notable. It tells me that the management team is running a more disciplined ship on expenses, even while traffic and discretionary demand remain soft.

That said, gross margin was down modestly for the year, pressured by tariffs, though underlying margin trends were healthier than the headline suggests. Shrink, which has been a major issue for TGT, improved back to pre-pandemic levels, and that delivered a sizable benefit. Inventory levels were also described as appropriate, which reduces the risk of heavy markdowns.

It’s worth noting that margin stability in the face of declining sales isn’t the same as true operating momentum. The core issue remains growth. Management is guiding to roughly 2% sales growth this year, with comps turning slightly positive, but that follows a prolonged stretch of stagnation.

So while I’ll give TGT credit for protecting profitability and controlling costs, the operating story still hinges on whether these investments can actually drive sustained top-line reacceleration.

What Is The Outlook For TGT Stock?

If Q4 was about stabilizing profitability, 2026 is about betting big on a return to growth.

During the call, TGT’s CEO Fiddelke rolled out a +$6B turnaround effort. This includes roughly +$2B in incremental P&L and CAPEX investment layered on top of an already sizable capital base. The plan targets stores, staffing, merchandising resets, technology, and category reinvestment, particularly in home, apparel, and grocery.

Fiddelke was pretty blunt in his commentary, saying that TGT “lost its way.” That level of self-awareness is encouraging, and investors initially applauded the plan, sending shares higher immediately following the release.

But optimism doesn’t change the math, in my view. Comps fell 2.6% for the year, traffic declined, and this marks the 13th straight quarter of weak or falling sales. Against those facts, the team is guiding to just above flat net sales growth and EPS of $7.50 to $8.50, with performance weighted to the back half of the year.

In other words, growth is expected, but modestly.

The investments are significant, and the strategic refocus on differentiation and speed makes sense. However, TGT is competing against operational machines like Walmart, Costco (COST), and Amazon (AMZN), all of which are currently benefiting from positions of strength.

Is TGT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At this stage, I view TGT as a classic “show me” story. The TGT team deserves credit for stabilizing profitability in the face of prolonged sales pressure, improving shrink back to pre-pandemic levels, and maintaining tighter inventory discipline.

Those are meaningful operational wins. However, cost control alone cannot power a sustained rerating of the stock, in my view. The core issue in my mind remains growth. After 13 consecutive quarters of flat or declining sales, I would say that investors would be forgiven for demanding tangible evidence that traffic and comps can turn sustainably positive.

In fairness to TGT, new CEO Michael Fiddelke is taking decisive action with a +$6B turnaround effort, and the focus on stores, staffing, merchandising, and technology is strategically sound.

Still, I believe these investments come with risk and the potential for near-term margin pressure. Meanwhile, competition from scaled operators like Walmart, Costco, and Amazon remains intense.

With shares trading near consensus price targets and growth still uncertain, I believe a neutral view is warranted. Until we see consistent top-line reacceleration and clear evidence that the turnaround is gaining traction, I am comfortable maintaining a neutral stance and viewing TGT stock as fairly valued at current levels.

data.title: StoneCo: I Am Doubling Down To Secure A Double-Digit Buyback Yield
data.description:
My investment in StoneCo (STNE) stock back in November 2021 has not gone that well. After 4+ years, I am sitting on a meager gain of ~4% on my initial investment while the S&P 500 has rallied almost 50% during this time. As much as I would have enjoyed a better performance, I understand that this is a part of being a long-term oriented, contrarian investor with a focus on small-caps. My last bullish piece on StoneCo was published in December 2025 when I thought its credit portfolio growth was fundamentally different from what we saw in 2021.

On March 2, StoneCo reported Q4 2025 earnings. In reaction to the earnings print, STNE stock plunged yesterday, losing almost 20% of its value. I believe StoneCo's long-term shareholders are now used to this substantial volatility associated with earnings reports. However, a 20% drop in just one trading session requires scrutiny. Although I am comfortable with increased volatility, that doesn't mean I am okay with sideways trading that lasts several years, which has been the case with StoneCo over the past 4+ years.

After diving deep into the Q4 earnings print, I have come across a few red flags that need to be monitored closely. That said, StoneCo is offering a buyback yield of at least 10.6% today, with the possibility of a yield as high as 26% if StoneCo fully utilizes its buyback potential this year. Given my belief that StoneCo has a long runway to grow, I have decided to add to my long position today to make the most of this very attractive buyback yield.

Q4 Earnings Recap

For the fourth quarter of 2025, StoneCo reported revenue of R$3.7 billion, up 13% YoY. The adjusted gross profit in Q4 was R$1.7 billion, up 9% YoY. For the full year, adjusted gross profit grew 13.5% YoY to R$6.32 billion, which was below management's guidance for R$6.375 billion. On the back of an adjusted EPS of R$2.87 in Q4 (up 27% YoY), full-year adjusted EPS came to R$9.71, ahead of the guidance.

Exhibit 1: Profitability accelerated in 2025

StoneCo's MSMB TPV reached R$135 billion in Q4, a YoY increase of just 5%. This saturating growth was probably one of the biggest reasons behind the post-earnings sell-off on March 3. MSMB client base, however, grew 15% YoY to 4.7 million.

The banking business continued to gain traction in Q4. Retail deposits reached R$11.1 billion, up 27% YoY. This stellar growth came on the back of a 21% YoY growth in banking active clients to 3.7 million.

The credit portfolio, which I discussed extensively in my previous article, expanded at a healthy pace in Q4 as well.

Exhibit 2: Credit portfolio metrics

Overall, StoneCo had a decent fourth quarter. However, there were some red flags hidden in the report.

Brazil is a fast-growing fintech market. According to IMARC Group, the fintech industry in Brazil is expected to grow at a CAGR of ~15% between 2026 and 2034. StoneCo, unfortunately, experienced a notable saturation in growth in 2025. TPV growth of ~5% is lackluster to say the least. This growth saturation could be a sign of intensifying competition. The market does not like it when high-growth companies succumb to competitive pressures.

Then there is the lingering risk of a potential credit portfolio deterioration. As I discussed extensively in my previous article, StoneCo cannot afford to repeat the same mistakes it made in 2021. Although I am comfortable with the growth of its credit portfolio, the Q4 report highlights a deterioration in credit quality. NPLs for 15-90 days increased from 3.12% in Q3 to 4.43% in Q4. This was a notable increase. Commenting on this adverse development, management highlighted that this stemmed from payment delays from a certain segment of its clients.

Not to forget, this credit quality deterioration came on the back of a 135% YoY growth in StoneCo's credit portfolio. What this means is that StoneCo is aggressively growing its credit portfolio at a time when challenging macro conditions are already having an impact on the credit quality of its portfolio. This strategy gives us flashbacks of what happened back in 2021. The good news is that management seems to focus on scaling the credit portfolio cautiously going forward.

Understanding The 2027 Gross Profit Guidance Slash

StoneCo slashed its gross profit guidance for 2027, which was another major driver of the 20% stock decline on March 3. StoneCo now expects 2027 adjusted gross profit to range between R$7.2 billion and R$8.3 billion. Previously, the company had guided for a low-end adjusted gross profit of R$10.2 billion.

Exhibit 3: Updated guidance for 2026 and 2027

This massive guidance slash comes on the back of two factors.

  1. The impact of the Linx divestiture.
  2. Operational headwinds.

Any guidance cut coming from the divestiture of Linx is nothing to be concerned about as it represents a strategic decision by the company to focus on its core business. As confirmed by CFO Diego Salgado on the earnings call, the Linx divestment is expected to have a R$2 billion impact on adjusted gross profit in 2027.

Even after accounting for this, there is another R$1 billion impact that is unaccounted for (based on the difference between the updated and previous lower end of the guidance for adjusted gross profit). This difference can be explained by the impact of operational headwinds. Management now has a more cautious view on TPV growth amid the challenges faced by small businesses in Brazil. This is a major challenge for growth. In addition to this, StoneCo seems to be caught offguard because of its massive presence among physical retailers at a time when online-only stores are taking over the retail space in Brazil. While this is a positive long-term development, StoneCo may face growth challenges in the short run.

Buybacks Create A Price Floor; Time To Double Down

StoneCo has been aggressively deploying capital for shareholder distributions through buybacks. In 2025, the company returned R$3 billion to shareholders via buybacks. For 2026, management has approved an R$2 billion buyback program. This comes to ~$378 million based on today's BRL/USD exchange rate of 5.28. In addition to this authorized repurchase program, StoneCo plans to distribute the proceeds it raised by divesting Linx to shareholders as well. Based on company filings, we are looking at another R$3 billion distribution as a result, or $568 million. Combined, the total buyback potential for this year comes to $946 million. StoneCo may decide to return a portion of this capital through a different mechanism, such as a special dividend, but the logic remains the same for our calculations here.

Exhibit 4: Extraordinary capital distribution plans

A quick calculation of the buyback yield reveals how attractive STNE is today.

To be on the conservative side, let's assume that StoneCo will only complete its authorized buyback program this year. Total buybacks will come to $378 million in this case. At the current market price of $13.73, StoneCo can repurchase ~27.5 million shares with this. In other words, the company can retire almost 11% of its float with its authorized buyback program this year. In reality, total buybacks are likely to be much higher given that the company has pledged to distribute Linx sale proceeds of $568 million. If its buyback potential is maximized, StoneCo can retire ~27% of its float at today's prices by repurchasing 69 million shares. Even if StoneCo's earnings remain flat this year, we are looking at the possibility of a much higher EPS thanks to the impact of these buybacks.

Based on the current market capitalization of $3.6 billion, below are the calculated buyback yields for each of these scenarios.

  1. Buyback yield based on the authorized repurchase program for 2026: 10.5%
  2. Buyback yield based on the full buyback potential for 2026: 26.3%

Even in the base-case scenario, we are looking at a very attractive buyback yield of 10.5%. In reality, though, the buyback yield could be as high as 26.3%.

I find this buyback yield quite attractive given my belief that the long-term outlook remains promising for StoneCo. As I have discussed in detail in my previous articles, there are many reasons behind this thinking, including the company's transition into a full banking platform, the data advantage enjoyed by the company thanks to its merchant payments business, and the favorable long-term industry outlook for the fintech sector in Latin America.

Risks

As I pointed out in an earlier segment, StoneCo's credit portfolio growth needs to be monitored carefully. A meaningful deterioration of credit quality will be punished badly. Investors are already getting flashbacks of StoneCo's 2021 debacle.

Competitive threats also need to be monitored. At a time when StoneCo is transitioning to a full banking business model, the last thing we want to see is its payments business losing market share to competitors. Recent earnings suggest the presence of intense competition.

I am also keeping an eye on how StoneCo's leadership change will impact the company's long-term growth strategy.

Takeaway

StoneCo stock crashed after reporting Q4 2025 earnings. The market was spooked by the company's gross profit guidance miss as well as the slashing of its 2027 guidance. Although there are some red flags, I still believe the long-term outlook is bright due to several reasons. The massive buyback yield has incentivized me to double down on STNE stock to make the most of this crash.

data.title: SYLD: Diversified Mid-Cap Value ETF Outperforming Competitors
data.description:
This article updates my review of May 2025 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

SYLD strategy

Cambria Shareholder Yield ETF (SYLD) is an actively managed ETF launched on 5/14/2013. SYLD has a portfolio of 99 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.17%, and an expense ratio of 0.59%. Dividends are paid on a quarterly basis. The fund invests in U.S. companies with high shareholder yield, defined as the combination of dividend cash payments, buybacks, and debt paydowns. The portfolio is co-managed by Mebane Faber, co-founder of Cambria Investment Management and author of Shareholder Yield, A Better Approach to Dividend Investing. The fund implements a quantitative strategy described as follows by Cambria in the prospectus:

Cambria selects the top 20% of stocks in the initial universe of U.S.-based, publicly listed companies based on their shareholder yield, as measured by dividend payments and net share buybacks. (...) Cambria’s quantitative algorithm then factors in the remaining stocks’ debt paydowns and applies a number of value metrics to create a composite, including metrics such as, but not limited to, price-to-book (P/B) ratio, price-to-sales (P/S) ratio, price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio, price-to-free cash-flow (P/FCF or P/CF) ratio, and enterprise multiple (EV/EBITDA). Cambria then selects the top 100 stocks for inclusion in the Fund’s portfolio that exhibit, in the aggregate, the best combination of shareholder yield characteristics and value metrics.

The fund tends to an equal-weight methodology, but weights may vary with market conditions and opportunities. The portfolio turnover rate was 42% in the most recent fiscal year and 50% in the previous year. As SYLD is classified by Morningstar in the Mid-Cap Value category, I will use as a benchmark the S&P MidCap 400 Index, represented by iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH).

Shareholder yield backtest

I ran a simulation on Portfolio123 to evaluate the performance of a ranking system based on shareholder yield, calculated as follows:

(Dividend Paid TTM + Equity Purchased TTM - Equity Issued TTM + Total Debt 12 months ago - Total Debt)/Market Capitalization

The simulation is run by ranking stocks of the Russell 1000 Index in equal weight in 10 buckets from lower to higher shareholder yields. The next chart plots the total return of the 10 buckets from 1999 to February 2026 with an annual rebalancing.

The higher the shareholder yield, the better the average total return in general, although buckets 5 to 7 are almost on par. This simulation validates the concept of shareholder yield as a valuable investing factor in the U.S. market based on 27 years of historical data.

Portfolio

SYLD has about 95% of its asset value in small and mid caps, with significant exposure in financials (22.7%), consumer discretionary (21.6%), energy (16.8%), and materials (13.1%). Other sectors are below 9%. Compared to IJH, SYLD overweights these top four sectors along with communication, while it greatly downplays industrials and technology, and ignores real estate and utilities.

The portfolio is well diversified, with low company-specific risk. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 13.6% of asset value, and the largest position weighs less than 2%.

Historical performance

SYLD has outperformed the mid-cap benchmark by 85 bps annualized since 5/21/2013. However, it shows a higher risk measured in drawdown and volatility, resulting in a slightly lower risk-adjusted performance (Sharpe ratio in the table below).

After a large drop in 2016, the annual sum of distributions has increased by 152% in nine years, greatly outpacing inflation (about 36% based on CPI). Nonetheless, it is still below the level of 2015.

Related funds

SYLD is part of a series of five shareholder yield ETFs, also including:

  • Cambria Foreign Shareholder Yield ETF (FYLD)
  • Cambria Emerging Shareholder Yield ETF (EYLD)
  • Cambria Micro & SmallCap Shareholder Yield ETF (MYLD)
  • Cambria Large Cap Shareholder Yield ETF (LYLD)

MYLD and LYLD were launched in 2024.

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics for SYLD, two shareholder yield ETFs, and the two largest mid-cap value ETFs:

  • Principal Value ETF (PY)

SYLD has the second-highest expense ratio. It comes in first position for total return since June 2017 but is second to SHRY for Sharpe ratio, due to significantly higher volatility. However, the very low assets under management ($18 million) and trading volumes of SHRY make its long-term survival uncertain.

Takeaway

Cambria Shareholder Yield ETF (SYLD) has a diversified portfolio of 99 stocks with high shareholder yield, a factor combining dividends, buybacks, and debt paydowns. SYLD has a focus on small and mid-caps, significant exposure in financials and consumer discretionary, and strong value characteristics. SYLD has outperformed a mid-cap benchmark since 2013 and several competitors since 2017. SYLD is well-suited for investors seeking long-term exposure to either mid-cap value or shareholder yield factor. Its main drawbacks are relatively high fees and volatility.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Battalion Oil: Surging Stock Price May Result In Preferred Share Conversion Into Common Shares
data.description:
Battalion Oil Corporation's (BATL) share price has soared recently to approximately $26 per share. It appeared to have attracted the attention of traders in late January 2026 after resolving its gas processing issues. That resulted in its trading volumes going from under 0.2 million shares on most days to 216 million shares on January 26, 2026.

The recent Iran news (and its impact on oil prices) has resulted in renewed interest in Battalion's stock, and its share price has gone up 6x in under a week.

Battalion is very overvalued now from a fundamental perspective, although this spike in its share price may result in an improvement to its overall situation through the conversion of some preferred shares into common shares, as well as the additional cash from an offering.

Full conversion of its preferred shares into common shares would increase the intrinsic value of its common shares from zero to around $4.15 per share.

Although I believe that Battalion's common stock is very overvalued currently, it is also quite difficult to find shares to short. There are also risks with shorting involving Battalion's small float and high cost to borrow, although I anticipate that its share price will end up significantly lower in the next few weeks as Battalion's common share count increases and traders move on to another stock.

Gas Processing Situation

Battalion entered into a JV agreement with Caracara Services in May 2022 to develop an acid gas treatment and carbon sequestration facility (later known as the Wink Amine Treater (WAT) AGI facility). Battalion retained a 5% equity interest in that JV, while Caracara provided the initial capital for the construction of the facility.

The WAT AGI facility ceased operations in August 2025, resulting in Battalion shutting in part of its Monument Draw production due to the impact of that facility shutdown on its sour gas treatment options.

Battalion then announced in January 2026 that it had entered into an agreement with a large-cap public midstream provider to process its gas at an alternate facility. The midstream provider recently expanded that facility and is able to essentially process all of Battalion's Monument Draw gas volumes.

This helps Battalion restore the Monument Draw production that it previously needed to shut in.

West Quito Draw Sale

West Quito Draw contributed around 2,700 BOEPD to Battalion's production in Q3 2022, and the PDP reserves there were previously estimated at 32% oil. Battalion hadn't done any development activities in West Quito Draw for a while but then brought two wells online in 2025. These two wells produced an average of 883 BOEPD per day per well during their first 120 days of production.

My rough estimate is that West Quito Draw can average around 2,700 BOEPD (35% oil) in net production in 2026, including the contributions from the new wells.

This would translate into around $15 million in 2026 EBITDA before G&A costs based on the commodity prices at the time Battalion entered into the sale agreement in mid-December 2025. Battalion ended up netting $60.1 million from its West Quito Draw sale, which would be approximately 4x EBITDA.

West Quito Draw had around 8 million BOE in proved reserves at the end of 2024, which was around 12% of Battalion's total proved reserves (although probably a higher proportion of its PDP reserves).

Battalion had $163 million in net debt at the end of Q3 2025, so this sale would reduce its net debt to around $103 million.

Battalion also recently announced that it was raising $15 million in gross proceeds ($14.1 million in net proceeds) from issuing common stock and common stock equivalents (prefunded warrants) to an institutional investor at a price of $5.50 per share. This would reduce its pro forma net debt further to $89 million.

Pro Forma 2026 Outlook

Pro forma for the West Quito Draw divestiture, I am modeling Battalion's production at 10,500 BOEPD, including 5,900 barrels per day of oil production.

At the current 2026 strip of roughly $68 to $69 WTI oil, Battalion may generate around $169 million in revenues net of hedges.

The Waha natural gas differential is expected to be over $3 for much of 2026, but Battalion has basis swaps for most of its 2026 natural gas production.

Type

Barrels/Mcf

$ Per Barrel/Mcf

$ Million

Oil

$68.00

NGLs

730,000

$22.00

Gas

Hedge Value

$4

Total

$169

I've assumed that Battalion spends around $55 million in capex to maintain production. Battalion is also making $40 million in mandatory term loan repayments from part of the cash proceeds from its West Quito Draw sale, which helps reduce its interest costs a bit.

This results in an estimate of $11 million in cash burn for Battalion in 2026.

$ Million

Lease Operating and Workover

$44

Production Taxes

$10

Cash G&A

$12

Gathering and Other

Cash Interest

$19

Capital Expenditures

$55

Total

$180

Battalion has around 70% of its projected 2026 post-divestiture oil production hedged, so its free cash flow this year may not change a huge amount even if oil prices massively increased.

Running the numbers at $100 WTI oil instead of $68 to $69 WTI oil would improve Battalion's projected 2026 free cash flow by $22 million, giving it around $11 million in 2026 free cash flow.

This doesn't include the PIK dividends on its preferred shares, which could add up to around $37 million in 2026 if they aren't converted into common shares.

Preferred Stock

Battalion had approximately $212 million in outstanding preferred stock at the end of Q3 2025. With a 16% PIK dividend rate, this would increase to around $229 million by the end of Q1 2026 and $258 million by the end of 2026.

As of the end of Q1 2026, the preferred stock could be converted into a total of approximately 31.9 million common shares. Full conversion would nearly triple Battalion's outstanding common share count, bringing that up to 48.3 million.

Full conversion in early March would result in a slightly lower increase to Battalion's share count (to around 47.9 million).

Although Battalion's share price has increased significantly, it doesn't appear that has an impact on Battalion's ability to force a conversion of the preferred stock into common stock. The mandatory conversion threshold is based on PDP PV-20 value (subject to various adjustments) as opposed to Battalion's stock price. I don't believe that flat $100 WTI oil would be enough to satisfy the mandatory conversion conditions.

With Battalion's current share price well above the conversion prices for its preferred shares, though, there is likely to be optional conversions by the preferred shareholders.

However, it is uncertain how many of the preferred shares will be converted into common shares (and sold) before Battalion's share price collapses. Some of the current short interest in Battalion is likely from preferred shareholders locking in a good price with the aim of converting their preferred shares into common to cover those shorts.

Notes On Valuation

As noted before, the West Quito Draw sale appears to be for approximately 4x EBITDA (excluding G&A). A similar 4x multiple for Battalion's remaining assets (also excluding G&A) would result in an estimated value of around $300 million at current 2026 strip prices. This would leave zero intrinsic value for Battalion's common shares after subtracting the value of its preferred shares and net debt.

A hypothetical scenario where all of Battalion's preferred shares were converted into common shares would result in an estimated intrinsic value of $4.15 per share. This would result in Battalion having approximately 50.6 million common shares (including the prefunded warrants).

Battalion is currently trading at well above this price, but I'd expect its share price to fall once a large number of shares are added to its float from the conversion of preferred shares followed by the sale of the new common shares (as well as its recent offering).

At $26 per share, Battalion's enterprise value would be approximately $1.4 billion with full conversion of its preferred shares into common stock. That would be approximately $133,000 per flowing BOE based on the estimated post-divestiture 2026 production of 10,500 BOEPD. A $1.4 billion valuation is also around 5x its estimated post-divestiture PDP PV-10 at mid-$70s WTI oil.

Conclusion

Battalion Oil has struggled for years with the PIK dividends on its preferred shares increasing the amount that is ahead of its common shares in its capital structure.

The recent surge in its share price may help alleviate that burden if it allows preferred shareholders to convert many of their preferred shares to common shares (with the likely intent of selling them).

A hypothetical scenario involving full conversion of the preferred shares would improve the intrinsic value of Battalion's common shares from zero to around $4.15 per share.

Battalion's current share price is being supported by the heavy trading of a small float (and a cost to borrow that has reached over 500%). The conversion of preferred shares could add substantially to Battalion's float, alleviating much of that pressure, though. Battalion also is adding around 2.7 million shares from its recent offering, so between the two, Battalion's outstanding common share count could go from 16.5 million to over 50 million.

data.title: Sandisk: I See A Path To $1000 Based On 2027 EPS Forecasts (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) has taken the market by storm, staging a face melting 1000% rally.

But the way I see it, this stock could quite easily double in the next 18 months, with $1000 a likely price target.

I reached this conclusion by running some forecasts based on earnings estimates and potential P/E ratios.

With the recent selloff, technicals are resetting, and I see a pretty clear accumulation structure.

Support at $480 is strong, and this would offer a perfect entry point

Sandisk looks Cheap Here

Yes, Sandisk has rallied over 1000% since its spinoff. I covered exactly why in my last article.

Not only is Sandisk a huge beneficiary of the memory shortage, but it is also potentially an even better play than Micron (MU)

But to understand this crazy rally, one only needs to look at the earnings estimates.

Sandisk is expected to grow earnings by 1000% in 2026, and then double EPS in 2027. This is crazy, and it makes valuing the company based on traditional fundamentals quite difficult, but we can certainly try.

In order to do this, let’s fast forward to June 2027, where EPS should be close to $80. At this point, growth should have normalized.

In fact, a consensus of 8 analysts has EPS declining by nearly 10% in 2028. But what would be a fair price at that point?

Scenario: $81 EPS 2027

Growth Rate Assumption

Implied P/E to have PEG=1

"Fair" Price

Current Market

103% (2027 Forecast)

~7x

$565

Conservative Case

20% (Long-term)

20x

$1,620

Analyst "High"

~12.5% (Relative)

12.5x

$1,012

This really depends on how we view growth longer term.

As I mentioned in my recent Micron article, PEG is perhaps one of the best metrics to see if a tech company is undervalued.

A fairly valued company should traditionally have a P/E ratio that matches the forecasted or recent growth rate, in order to balance out the PEG at 1.

At today’s price and forecast, as we can see above, this implies SNDK would trade at a 7 PE in 2027. But this doesn’t seem realistic at all.

Even if growth does slow down for a year or two, I believe Sandisk, like Micron, is benefiting from a secular growth trend.

Assuming a longer-term growth rate of 20% and P/E of 20, gives us a target price of $1,620. This is likely on the higher side.

Ultimately, if we apply a P/E of around 12, which is slightly below today’s forward PE of 1/5, we get a target price of just over $1000.

This is in line with the price target from UBS, and very reasonable given comparable industry multiples and the current valuation.

Again, this could vary a lot depending on how growth looks after two years, but this is fairly conservative P/E in my opinion.

Technicals Are Re-Setting

The other key development since my last update is that we’re seeing a bullish consolidation and resetting of the technicals.

While the broader market sells off, Sandisk is holding in a tight range, consolidating as it did back in Q4 of 2025. Meanwhile, the daily RSI is back near neutral levels.

Sandisk has broken below the 20 EMA, and it looks ready to test support just above $500, which is the 50 EMA and an area of volume support.

This will likely be an ideal entry point, just like it was back in November.

Risks

But even though Sandisk looks like a slam dunk, there are still considerable investment risks.

Sandisk is a newly spun-off corporation, and there’s still a lot we don't know about the business and management.

We also have significant execution risk, and with expectations so high, any miss could really lead to a re-rating of the company.

To add to this, market sentiment around AI has been pretty bearish in the last few months, and we may need to see a wider correction before we can see a reversal and new highs.

Focusing on the technicals, it's worth noting that if we do break below the 50 EMA, we don’t have much support until the 200 EMA at $250, so there’s some potential downside and volatility ahead.

Final Thoughts

I really like Sandisk at this price, and I believe this stock could easily double in the next 18 months.

This makes sense when we look at valuation, and there’s room for this to go even beyond $1000.

The technicals also look bullish to me, for the time being, but this could quickly change.

data.title: Surviving 'Epic Fury' And The Asian Stock Market Crash
data.description:
Colonel Kilgore enjoyed the smell of morning napalm in "Apocalypse Now" since it reflected triumph. Today on institutional trading floors on Wall Street, the air reeks of volatility, but only a faint scent of margin calls seems to be burning off traders’ terminal screens. Not only did “Operation Epic Fury” in the Middle East destroy Iran's radar systems, it also destroyed tens of billions of dollars in leveraged bets, eliminating the illusion of comfort that had permeated the markets. Spring Cleaning (Also applicable since we are entering the warmest season). There are two different stories told by the collapsing of Asian markets and the perceived/illusory strength of U.S. stocks.

Here I will attempt to connect the dots. I may go against the mainstream media, but this is what I believe. I am employed by an asset management company and have access to high-level market data through Bloomberg Terminals and reporting from sell-side analysts from all around the world. However, I always form my own opinion, using capital flow to differentiate between fundamentals and algorithmic hysteria. I strive to remain objective and do not want to sell you one story versus the other here, just the facts.

The Bureaucratic Blockade and the “Crude Tax"

Stock exchanges are typically impervious to the threat of geopolitical crises. We observed this between 2023 and 2025 and again at the beginning of 2026 with the Venezuelan and Greenlandian crises. Stock exchanges are generally apathetic towards conflict or wars until it affects an economy’s ability to generate cash flow. Pure geopolitics will never result in a bear market. The "preventive strike" on Iran's nuclear and command structure immediately resulted in retaliatory strikes by Iran on Qatar, Bahrain, and Oman. These retaliations resulted in a disruption of the worldwide energy balance that was already delicately balanced.

The most destructive weapon of economic warfare in this crisis is bureaucracy. I covered this in my last two analyses (Part I and Part II). The Strait of Hormuz, a 39-kilometer channel through which 21% of the world’s total petroleum consumption (21 million barrels per day) is delivered, is now blocked. 7 Major Marine Insurance Associations have withdrawn coverage for War Risk in the Persian Gulf. Not a single shipping company is willing to take their tankers across the Persian Gulf as long as they do not have insurance. This "Administrative Blockade" has created a floating parking lot for over 700 ships.

Late yesterday afternoon, the president promised a U.S. Navy escort for tankers. That caused a small drop in oil price when the news was released but did little to assuage institutional investors. While an armed escort protects against a physical attack, it does nothing to stop an attacking drone from starting a fire at an operating refinery.

In just 48 hours, Brent crude has increased 12%, to $82/barrel. Goldman Sachs estimates that a prolonged closure would add a $15 premium to the structural fair value of a barrel of oil. If oil stays above $100/bbl, then trucking logistics costs will increase by anywhere from 10% to 18%. The margin compression of corporations will be taxed heavily either through destruction of demand or the burn rate of revenue through absorbing those costs.

Bloomberg Economics produced a chart showing how severe this would be. For example, if we were to have a prolonged, high-intensity conflict, the price of crude oil could go to $108/bbl. At that price, any reasonable model of multiple expansion that is currently supporting the current bull cycle would cease to exist.

Asian Massacre and the AI Bubble

When Western investors were focused on the Middle East, the biggest financial crash was taking place in Seoul, Tokyo, and Hong Kong. The iShares MSCI Pacific ex-Japan ETF (EPP) dropped up to 4.5 percent during one day, while the Korean Stock Exchange (Kospi Index) plummeted an incredible 7.2 percent in a single trading session. A week later, the Kospi index would fall another 6.2 percent, eliminating $430 billion in market value in less than two days.

In retail investor circles, it is commonly stated that the reason for the collapse was Asia's dependency on imported petroleum products. Data on institutional flow activity reveals the opposite. Both Japan and China have sufficient hydrocarbon reserves to maintain domestic economic stability for at least several months, and in the case of China, possibly even one full year. It is not a problem with petroleum products that caused the collapse, but the most severe speculative bubble since the last major speculative bubble occurred at the end of the dot-com era.

Country Day
Japan 254
South Korea 208
China 200
India 74
Thailand 61
Philippines 60
Indonesia 20
Vietnam 15
Cambogia No Data

Before the war, investors worldwide flocked into North Asian stocks, particularly those of companies in the AI hardware space, specifically Samsung Electronics Co. (SSNLF), SK Hynix Inc. (HXSCF), etc., with price expectations that these would continue to grow indefinitely.

These funds continued to be poured into the space. The iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) received an unprecedented amount of money as it recorded the largest outflows in a 5-day period in its 25-year history of $1.77 billion before the war. Retail investors were highly risky and also pushed margin borrowing to record levels because of a very loose lending environment.

Hostilities broke out and were the trigger that set off the energy price rise, which supported the U.S. dollar (DXY), pushed up interest rates, and thus put the leveraged positions of Asian speculative investors at risk. Once banks started issuing merciless margin calls, they cut off the liquidity that allowed their trading accounts to be able to recover from the losses caused by these margin calls. So the winners in the semiconductor trade dumped their holdings for a profit.

These trades, as shown in the Kospi chart above, represent an example of what is known as "algorithmic dysfunction." These are not simply a result of a "technical retracement," but rather the forced liquidation of all the crowded positions taken on by investors who were long in semiconductors and short in software and were unable to meet their margin calls. And because the long and short trades were done simultaneously, there was "double damage" done to their portfolios.

The Death of Safe Havens and the Dominance of the Dollar

With global financial markets becoming so complex that there are fewer places to hide than ever before, finding a safe haven has become an act of faith rather than an eventuality. The financial dynamics caused by the current Middle East crisis have turned what were once considered safe havens into liquidity traps, except for one.

U.S. Treasury Prices Plummet While Yields Soar

iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) prices plummeted as the yields on U.S. Treasuries rose sharply. The 10-Year Treasury rate broke the 4.06% barrier and closed at 4.11%. The two-year treasury suffered a sharp 12 basis point rise to 3.59%.

The yields on these U.S. Treasury securities increased during the crisis because of two factors:

  • The fear of inflation: When there is a large rise in energy costs, the actual or real value of a fixed-rate bond decreases. For example, if the price of crude oil increases by 50%, then it is estimated that the rate of inflation in the United States will increase by approximately 1.4 percent (the increase in the European Union will be slightly less at 1.1 percent, and in Japan at 1.2 percent). Therefore, when there is an expectation of increasing inflation, the Federal Reserve Board does not have the option of cutting interest rates to help purchase U.S. government securities.
  • Increased fiscal risk and deficit: The energy crisis has also been exacerbated by a domestic political concern for the U.S. administration. The U.S. Supreme Court decided against President Donald Trump's suggested tariffs, therefore lowering the predicted tax income suggested by the White House. Additionally, with the prospect of additional military spending due to the growing Asian and Middle Eastern conflicts, there is a greater fear among investors of an uncontrollable national debt and deficit, and therefore, a significant decrease in the attractiveness of U.S. government securities.

Gold and Silver

Gold has always represented a perfect example of a safe haven, but its behavior toward this economic shock has been volatile, unpredictable, and erratic – three traits no investor would ever want to see in a hedge product.

  • The price of gold initially surged upward from the time of the announcement to a high of $5,419 an ounce before collapsing back down to $5,261 and recovering slightly to close at $5,337 per ounce; however, the volatility experienced by silver was far worse than that of gold as it dropped approximately 7 percent.
  • Gold falls during war because the price of gold has grown so financially sensitive that the commodity has become an ideal target for both tactical speculation and opportunistic investment strategies. During times of major equity market risk-off, as we have witnessed with the collapse of Asian markets, speculative positions are being sold out due to margin calls resulting from a lack of liquidity. The funds selling their positions are doing so based on need rather than choice. Since gold and silver had reached all-time highs and provided massive returns, these commodities became prime targets for selling off to raise cash to meet margin calls resulting from the collapse of technology equities. Historically, gold and silver were considered non-correlated assets; however, due to the extreme amount of money being raised through the sale of gold and silver in order to raise cash to meet margin calls, the two assets have exhibited a lethal positive correlation. When liquidity becomes extremely tight, all assets will experience significant declines.

The Hegemony of the Dollar King (The Last Bastion)

The U.S. Dollar has become the only remaining unchallenged source of global life support.

Wall Street's False Security

While the Eastern front was undergoing bloodshed, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) held steady and demonstrated unrelenting strength. Not only did the U.S. equity market survive the assault, it also triggered a ferocious rebound. The S&P 500 rallied 0.7%, while the Nasdaq 100 surged 1.3%, and was primarily led by tech stocks.

There were two catalysts for the buyers.

  1. Rumors that Iran may have initiated diplomatic overtures with the intent to broker a cease-fire to the conflict (subsequently denied).
  2. the "Goldilocks" macroeconomic numbers. The U.S. ISM Services Index, which had been expanding at a faster-than-expected rate since mid-2022, jumped to 56.1 for the highest growth rate in years.

Historically, the other major risk factor to the financial markets is monetary. Every $10 per barrel increase in crude oil has historically pushed the CPI upward approximately 20-30 basis points. Since the CPI remains stubbornly high at 2.7% - 2.9%, a new shock to energy prices effectively limits the ability of the Fed to take action.

Bottom Line

If the oil price actually approaches $100, we have a very serious problem.

A severe lesson is provided by the forced sector rotation in Asia: In extremely polarized markets, the greatest risk is associated with extreme positioning. The second round effects from inflation of energy prices have still not been accounted for by Wall Street. Betting on an intraday rebound of the S&P 500 by betting on a Fed rescue is a mathematical loser because inflation has eliminated the possibility of a Fed rate cut.

I would recommend holding off from investing in the S&P 500 at this time. As I have said in many analyses, the most beneficial thing you can do right now is "do nothing" and wait. There is simply no justification for committing additional capital at current multiples with both a compressed equity risk premium and increasing real interest rates.

Margin calls have briefly destroyed Asian Markets, but after that, we can identify long-term strategic entry points from the price dislocations that will arise once selling volumes normalize. Waiting is my personal choice.

Liquidity isn't a cost; rather, I think you should now view it as the only tactical (defensive) tool to take advantage of the price anomalies caused by the forced liquidation of assets in the coming quarters. Many investors think central banks will come to their rescue. It's possible, and I don't think the Fed will ever make another 2008-style mistake. Regardless, I don't want to suffer this volatility; rather, I want to manage it.

What about you?

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia: Abnormal Returns Are A Thing From The Past
data.description:
Last week, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) released its Q4 2026 earnings, and most market participants were up in arms with the negative share price response. To many the combination of a strong quarterly report and a share price drop was interpreted as a strong buying opportunity and hopes that the market is somehow being irrational about one of the most followed companies of our day.

After all, NVDA had yet another record-breaking quarter and the stock is still down by around 6% over the past 5-day period.

It might be disappointing seeing the underlying business prosper while the stock price is being essentially flat over the past 6-month period, but that's the reality when investing in stocks that are trading at prohibitively high multiples.

The Q4 report and the reaction that followed were an exact repetition from Nvidia's previous report, which did not translate into a meaningful boost for the share price in spite of the strong revenue growth and margin improvements. That is why, back in November, I warned that investors should distinguish between Nvidia's as a business and NVDA share price.

The Telling Signs

If we zoom-out by just one year, then the recent drop of NVDA looks less as an irrational movement and more as a normal course of events after results got priced-in about a year earlier.

As a matter of fact, earnings growth in FY 2026 is roughly in-line with NVDA share price performance over the same period.

Earnings surprises above the analysts' estimates have also come down gradually in recent years and over the past few quarters have been at mid-single digits which suggests that the market forecasts are now aligned.

Another telling sign that investors should not take NVDA share price rally in the first half of calendar 2025 as a given was the major drop in trading volumes. This is usually associated with lack of conviction and a potential pullback later on as investors adjust their views.

As a result, it is not surprising that we now see NVDA trading at exactly the same multiples from a year ago. This does not indicate that the business performance is worsening, but rather that the market is now in a much better position to accurately forecast earnings growth in the coming year.

Strong Narratives, Predictable Performance

What instantly became clear during the earnings release was that there's no slowdown of Nvidia's business momentum. The company remains as the "Inference King" with no immediate threat of competition and sustained demand from data centers.

Demand for Blackwell architecture remains strong with even older architectures showing encouraging results which is a clear sign that the sector is still faced with temporary capacity constrains which could often result in short-term abnormalities in terms of pricing.

Q4 data center revenue of $62 billion increased 75% year-over-year and 22% sequentially, driven primarily by sustained strength in Blackwell and the Blackwell Ultra ramp. With NVIDIA infrastructure in high demand, even Hopper and much of the 6-year-old Ampere-based products are sold out in the cloud.

Source: NVDA Q4 2026 Earnings Transcript.

Nvidia's management also highlighted the growing opportunity in networking, which is an essential part of the data center business. Revenue in networking grew by a staggering 350% during the last quarter and that's impressive, albeit once again - mostly anticipated at this point. CEO Jen-Hsun Huang hinted that NVDA will be leveraging its competitive positioning in the space and that's definitely good news for investors.

And I think that we're probably the largest Ethernet networking company in the world today and surely will be soon.

Source: NVDA Q4 2026 Earnings Transcript.

Recent quarterly results from Cisco (CSCO) have also shown a notable pickup in demand from hyperscalers for networking solutions.

AI infrastructure orders taken from hyperscalers totalled $2.1 billion in Q2 compared to $1.3 billion just last quarter and equal to the total orders taken in all of fiscal year '25, marking another significant acceleration in growth across our silicon, systems and optics.

Source: CSCO Q2 2026 Earnings Transcript.

As a result, NVDA's guidance for the current quarter involves a total revenue of $78bn, up from $44bn in the prior period.

This assumes around 75% year-on-year growth in the first quarter of fiscal year 2027 and right now that's above the consensus forward revenue growth estimate of 52%.

This divergence might seem counterintuitive, especially when we consider that so far hyperscalers' capital expenditures show no signs of cooling off in the first months of 2026.

Analyst expectations for 2026 CapEx across the top 5 cloud providers and hyperscalers who collectively account for a little over 50% of our data center revenue are up nearly $120 billion since the start of the year and approaching $700 billion. We continue to expect the transition of classic data center workloads to GPU accelerated computing and the use of AI to enhance today's hyperscale workloads and contribute toward roughly half of our long-term opportunity.

Source: NVDA Q4 2026 Earnings Transcript.

The broader expectations are for hyperscalers capex to increase considerably in 2026, but many investors are already looking ahead for what's to come after that peak in spending.

So far, on an aggregated quarterly basis, we do not see any major red flags and Q1 2026 numbers will be much anticipated after overall spend in the last quarter of 2025 noted a slight cool-off to 71% year-on-year growth.

Some analysts are pointing to the hyperscalers' growing needs to finance their growth through debt as internally generated cash flow is no longer enough fuel the rapid development of computing capacity.

That's hardly a reason to be concerned given the strong balance sheet of these companies, but it is a sign that the current spending spree is unlikely to grow further and capex growth would have to come down in the coming years.

The main issue for NVDA here is that even a slight ease in capex growth by the hyperscalers could have major consequences. The now lower price/earnings multiple does take into account the increased risk of slower revenue growth. A P/E multiple of 22 on a forward basis might appear quite low for a company like NVDA, but that low multiple could be misleading.

The extraordinary increase in capex by the hyperscalers last year and the lack of spare capacity by the semiconductor foundries has resulted in significant price increases for Nvidia chips. That is why, the commentary extract above that even Hopper and Ampere-based products are selling quite well is not surprising.

Thus, it is reasonable to assume that NVDA gross profitability is temporary elevated until the shortages are resolved. Right now gross margins have settled at around 75% (see the graph below) and most recent guidance for Q1 2027 confirmed that the prior highs of 78% and above are unlikely to be achieved again. Fixed cost efficiencies have allowed NVDA to retain its operating margins close to record-highs, but this might not last if gross margins continue to normalize in the coming years.

The market is now preemptively taking measures against the very high probability scenario of NVDA gross margins normalizing in the coming years. While operating margins are unlikely to fall very steeply on the back of fixed cost efficiencies, they will be subject to pressure too. In such an event, NVDA's EPS growth would suffer tremendously, even if revenue growth remains strong.

Whether or not the current and forward P/E multiples accurately reflect what's to come is debatable, but given the risks outlined above a forward multiple of 22 does not necessarily mean that NVDA is undervalued. More likely than not, we will witness more volatility in calendar 2026, but by taking pre-emptive measures against margin pressures and expected cool-off of hyperscalers' capex growth is now the most sensible course action by the market.

Conclusion

Nvidia's share price drop following the latest quarterly release was both anticipated and sensible. The stock's strong rally in the first half of 2025 has already accounted for years of growth, without properly addressing some risks. Market participants are already looking ahead and anticipating a degree of normalization when it comes to cloud infrastructure spending and this is now being reflected within NVDA's performance. While volatility is to be expected in 2026, investors should get used to the stock's lower valuation multiples.
